coredata.dc:title: Regulating optoelectronics of carbon dots with redox-active dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Panigrahi, Aradhana
Behera, Ranjan Kumar
Mishra, Leepsa
Dubey, Priyanka
Dutta, Soumi
Sarangi, Manas Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The electron transfer (ET) processes in carbon-based quantum dots (CDs) have laid to extensive research endeavors due to their tunable optoelectronic properties with alteration in surface functionalization, doping, surface charge, etc. Here, we proffer shreds of evidence on pH-dependent electron transfer and transport properties of undoped CDs (UCDs) and amine-doped CDs (ACDs) with a redox-active neurotransmitter, dopamine (DA). A pronounced pH-dependent photoluminescence (PL) quenching is depicted for both the CDs with DA, where the quenching efficiency substantially increases in alkaline solvents. Taking advantage of the structural transformation of DA from hydroquinone to quinone, the electron accepting capacity can be improved for alkaline pH, which enhances the ET efficiency. The pH-regulated conductance measurements across the metal-CD-metal junction reveal a hike in the conductivity for acidic and alkaline pH relative to the neutral one. The I-V traces of UCDs and ACDs are contributed by an initial linear current rise and non-linear growth at higher bias explicating both direct and Fowler-Nordheim (F-N) tunneling mechanisms. In addition, the incorporation of DA into these CDs, not only significantly increases conductance in all pH media but also enables them to tunnel the potential barrier directly in acidic media, reducing F-N tunneling. These results could provide new avenues for developing reliable optoelectronic devices and sensing probes for a wide range of applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electron transfer
Carbon dots
Dopamine
Conducting atomic force microscopy
Tunneling mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: AP, PD, and SD acknowledge fellowships from IIT Patna. MKS acknowledges the funding received from the science and engineering research board (SERB) through project CRG/2022/004870. The authors also acknowledge FIST (SR/FST/PS-1/2018/34) for the AFM facility, Mr. Nishant Kumar, Ms. Anwesha Mahapatra, and Ms. Priyanka Priyadarshani Samal for their help in C-AFM measurements.

AP, PD, and SD acknowledge fellowships from IIT Patna. MKS acknowledges the funding received from the science and engineering research board (SERB) through project CRG/2022/004870 . The authors also acknowledge FIST ( SR/FST/PS-1/2018/34) for the AFM facility, Mr. Nishant Kumar, Ms. Anwesha Mahapatra, and Ms. Priyanka Priyadarshani Samal for their help in C-AFM measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM micrographs of UCDs (a), ACDs (b), and insets showing their size distribution, their FTIR spectra (c) assigned by different functional groups shown in colored regions, absorption spectra (d), emission (solid line) and excitation (dotted line) spectra (e), and lifetime decay profiles (f) of UCDs (red) and ACDs (violet) with λex = 377 nm, λem = 463 nm, 455 nm, respectively.

Time-resolved PL decay curves of UCDs (upper panel) and ACDs (lower panel) with the sequential addition of DA from 0-0.9 mM with an interval of 0.1mM at pH-3 (a, d), pH-7 (b, e), and pH-10 (c, f). The grey dotted line in each figure displays the IRF. The inset figures show the PL emission with the continuous addition of DA with the same concentration as the lifetime decay curves at the corresponding pH values. The UCDs and ACDs are excited here at an excitation wavelength of 377 nm.

Stern-Volmer (S-V) plots of UCDs (upper panel) and ACDs (lower panel) with an increase in the concentration of DA from 0-0.9 mM, plotted from PL intensities (a, c), and average lifetime values (b, d) at different pH's. The insets show the colors corresponding to different pH values.

pH-dependent I-V traces of UCDs (upper panel) and ACDs (lower panel), respectively. I-V curves of UCDs and ACDs in the absence (sphere) and presence of DA (star) at pH-3 (a purple; d, dark cyan), pH-7 (b, red; e, violet), and pH-10 (c, olive; f, wine), respectively. The orange and black lines in these curves represent the fitting lines fitted with direct tunneling and F-N tunneling equations, respectively.

The effect of pH and the presence of DA on the tunneling mechanism. The F-N plots for UCDs (upper panel) and ACDs (lower panel) at pH-3 (a purple; d, dark cyan), pH-7 (b, red; e, violet), and at pH-10 (c, olive; f, wine) in the absence (sphere) and presence (star) of DA (0.9 mM), respectively. The higher bias region is fitted to a linear equation showing F-N tunneling (black line) and the lower bias region is fitted to the exponential growth equation (direct tunneling, orange line).

Schematic illustration of the plausible electron transfer mechanism between the CDs and DA in both acidic and alkaline pH media deciphering the role of pH (a), the interaction between DA hydroquinone and UCDs (b), and ACDs (c).

head.ce:title: Regulating optoelectronics of carbon dots with redox-active dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna, India 801106
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna, India 801106

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electron transfer (ET) processes in carbon-based quantum dots (CDs) have laid to extensive research endeavors due to their tunable optoelectronic properties with alteration in surface functionalization, doping, surface charge, etc. Here, we proffer shreds of evidence on pH-dependent electron transfer and transport properties of undoped CDs (UCDs) and amine-doped CDs (ACDs) with a redox-active neurotransmitter, dopamine (DA). A pronounced pH-dependent photoluminescence (PL) quenching is depicted for both the CDs with DA, where the quenching efficiency substantially increases in alkaline solvents. Taking advantage of the structural transformation of DA from hydroquinone to quinone, the electron accepting capacity can be improved for alkaline pH, which enhances the ET efficiency. The pH-regulated conductance measurements across the metal-CD-metal junction reveal a hike in the conductivity for acidic and alkaline pH relative to the neutral one. The I-V traces of UCDs and ACDs are contributed by an initial linear current rise and non-linear growth at higher bias explicating both direct and Fowler-Nordheim (F-N) tunneling mechanisms. In addition, the incorporation of DA into these CDs, not only significantly increases conductance in all pH media but also enables them to tunnel the potential barrier directly in acidic media, reducing F-N tunneling. These results could provide new avenues for developing reliable optoelectronic devices and sensing probes for a wide range of applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The datas are provided in the manuscript and supplementary materials

ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the efficient charge carriers generation and separation, charge transfer in low-dimensional nanomaterials has been at the forefront for optimal applications in photo-catalysis [1], energy storage [2], sensing [3], photovoltaics [4], light-emitting diodes [5], single molecular optoelectronics [6,7], etc. The charge transfer rate as explained by Marcus' theory [8] relies on the donor-acceptor distance, the energy levels of the donor and acceptor, the driving force, solvent reorganization energy, electronic coupling between them, etc [9]. In photovoltaic and optoelectronics, the extent of charge transfer, followed by carrier separation often determines the effectiveness of the device [10,11]. Electron transfer (ET) in semiconducting carbon dots (CDs) has emerged as adequate photo-active material for various optoelectronic applications due to their unique properties like inexpensive synthesis, stability, solution processivity, less toxicity, etc [12]. Factors like surface functionalization [13], precursor variation, doping [14], surface charge [15], confinement [16], bandgap, etc. in CDs provide additional opportunities to modulate their ET properties [17]. For instance, doping with boron in CDs encourages the hole transfer to phthalocyanine, while the ET mechanism is enhanced by phosphorus doping with the same acceptor [18]. Tweaking the surface chemistry of CDs with additional interfacial charges has also been observed to modulate the ET process through Coulombic interactions [14,15]. Here, we have illustrated the ET behavior of photoexcited CDs with the neurotransmitter dopamine (DA) when subjected to doping and alteration in pH. The rationale for choosing the redox-active DA for this study stems from the fact that the optical properties of DA are sensitive to external pH. The surrounding pH causes the structural interconversion of DA from its quinone (basic pH) to hydroquinone form (acidic pH), which can strain its electron-accepting or donating capabilities [19]. This distinctive behavior of DA with pH has been reported to perturb the ET process in various QDs such as graphene QDs [20] and CdSe/ZnS QDs [21,22]. Focusing on such competence, Medintz et al. have characterized the pH-dependent photoluminescence (PL) quenching in a QDs-DA bio-conjugate for charge transfer-coupled intracellular pH sensing application [23]. The detection of DA with pH variation is reported by Chatterjee et al. revealing the ET from Boron and Sulfur co-doped graphene QDs to oxidation-induced DA-quinone in alkaline conditions [24]. In this context, the effect of pH variation in doped and undoped CDs needs more clarification for an intuitive understanding of the ET mechanism in CDs-based composite systems. The importance of such a pH-dependent study is not limited to the investigation of ET dynamics, rather it has strong implications in single-molecular electronics devices as well. Scullion et al. have demonstrated the pH-controlled conductance across a single molecular junction with the alteration of both conformation and bridge length of a peptide single wire [25]. They have observed that the pH-induced extension of molecular configuration lowers the conductance, offering a long-range ET between two electrodes. Herrer et al. have claimed that the conductance of a pyrazole derivative gets enhanced four times due to the stronger electronic coupling between the gold tip and deprotonated pyrazole (pyrazolate) moiety, when the medium is changed from normal to basic pH [26]. Considering the advent in the molecular electronics, the importance of surrounding pH and presence of redox active molecules on the charge transport properties at the single molecular level demands further attention.

In this study, we endeavor to comprehend the significance of doping and the role of solvent pH on charge migration through the quantum-confined CDs using both spectroscopic and conducting atomic force microscopic (C-AFM) techniques. C-AFM is one of the most appealing approaches for studying electrical properties by visualizing the sample topography, with quantification of the tunneling current as a function of applied voltage [27,28]. With a focus on the ET mechanism at a single molecular level, we scrutinize the impact of pH-controlled interactions of undoped CDs (UCDs) and ethylene diamine (EDA)-doped CDs (ACDs) with DA and their alteration to optical and electronic conductivity. As observed from the spectroscopic results, both the ET rate and efficiency are higher with the increase in surrounding pH (3 to 10) for both CDs. The conductivity of the CDs, as observed from C-AFM, increases significantly for acidic pH in comparison to neutral and alkaline pH. This follows the direct tunneling only, while both direct and Fowler-Nordheim (F-N) tunneling in other cases. The inclusion of DA in these CDs abruptly increases their conductivity, emphasizing the importance of ET-induced current conduction.

Citric acid (CA, ≥99.5%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, about 35%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥97%) are bought from Merck, and ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥99%), and dopamine hydrochloride are bought from Sigma Aldrich, used without additional purification. Deionized (DI) water is taken as a solvent for performing all the experiments.

UCDs and ACDs are synthesized using the pyrolysis method. Briefly, 1 g of CA is added to 2 mL of DI water. When CA is completely mixed in DI water, it is heated and stirred at 250 °C with 500 rpm for 5 h. In between the reaction process, DI water is added dropwise to avoid the burning of CA. After 5 h, a yellow color sticky material is formed confirming the formation of UCDs. Following a similar process as that of UCDs, ACDs are prepared with pyrolyzing CA for one more hour with EDA. Then the solution becomes brownish, forming ACDs. A pictorial representation of the synthesis protocol of these CDs is given in Scheme S1. Then, the column chromatography method is used for the purification of both UCDs and ACDs. After purification, both the CDs are stored for future experimentation.

All UV-visible absorption spectra are recorded using a JASCO (V-770) spectrophotometer, and the PL excitation and emission spectra are measured using a Fluoromax-4 fluorometer. All these measurements are done by using 1 cm path length quartz cuvettes. Using a PerkinElmer Spectrum 400 spectrometer in the attenuated total reflection mode, Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra of these CDs are obtained. All the steady-state experiments are carried out at ambient conditions.

A Horiba TCSPC spectrometer with a picosecond pulsed laser diode (377 nm) and a microchannel plate-photomultiplier tube (MCP-PMT) detector is used to study the excited state lifetime measurements [29–31]. The obtained data are analyzed using EzTime 3.2.9.6 data analyzer software after deconvoluting the Instrument Response Function (IRF) and are fitted with multi-exponential functions as given in Eq. 1. Origin 8.5 software is used to plot all the decay curves. The average decay lifetimes are fitted and calculated using the following formulas,
(1)

( t )

∑

τ i 2
∑
i = 1
p
A i
τ i
where Ai’s are the contributions corresponding to the lifetime components τi’s, respectively, and τavg is the average lifetime of these CDs and p = 3 and 2 for UCDs and ACDs, respectively.

The ET rate can be calculated both from lifetime values by Eq. 2 and PL intensity values by using the S-V equation as given in
(3a)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V

(3b)

where F0 and τ0 are the PL intensity and average lifetime of UCDs and ACDs without the quencher (DA) respectively, and F and τ are the corresponding values with DA. KSV is the rate of quenching constant [32].

The ET efficiency (

) between UCDs/ACDs and DA can be calculated as,
(4)

τ 0

A JEOL-F200 TEM running at 200 kV is used to capture the TEM images of both CDs. Both CDs are diluted in DI water to prepare the samples for imaging. The samples are then drop-cast over a carbon-coated copper grid and dried under vacuum. The TEM images are analyzed using ImageJ software.

Using the non-contact mode of AFM, topography images of both the CDs are acquired using a Bruker Multi-mode 8 setup. The preparation method for samples is described below. We have taken silicon wafers as the substrates for non-contact imaging. First, the silicon substrates are cleaned with acetone followed by sonication for 5 minutes and then the same procedure was continued with DI water. Then diluted samples of the CDs are spin-coated on silicon substrates at 500 rpm for 60 seconds. All the samples for imaging are prepared at ambient environmental conditions and the AFM images are interpreted using WSxM 5.0 developer 8.3 software.

All the C-AFM measurements are recorded using the contact mode of operation, where a PtIr-coated SCM-PIT AFM cantilever (spring constant – 2.8 N/m, resonance frequency – 75 kHz) is used. The samples for conducting measurements are prepared by spin coating these CDs over the substrate of Highly Oriented Pyrolytic Graphite (HOPG) by using the following steps. First, a few layers of HOPG are lifted off and adhered on a glass slide with the help of double-sided tape. Then, the CDs are diluted in DI water and spin-coated on the as-prepared HOPG (1000 rpm, 60 s). After keeping the samples for drying, the electrical measurements are performed with current sensitivity of 1 nA/V. These I-V curves are analyzed using origin 8.5 software and are fitted using direct and F-N tunneling equations as given below.

Direct and F-N tunneling mechanisms can be explained by the equations given below,
(5a)

where Aeff is the effective contact area, m, and m* are the rest mass and effective mass of an electron, particle size is d (nm),
φ B
represents the barrier height (eV), the electronic charge is q with Planck's constant (h), applied voltage (V), and current (I) through the junction.

Eqs. 5a and 5b can be rewritten as follows,

Factors like doping and surface modification in CDs bring pronounced alterations in their photophysics [33,34]. Herein, highly fluorescent semiconducting UCDs and ACDs are synthesized and characterized through multiple spectroscopic and microscopic techniques (Fig. 1 ). To obtain information about the structure, size, and morphology of these CDs, TEM and AFM measurements are conducted. The TEM images of UCDs and ACDs in Fig. 1 a and b depict their spherical shapes, as also reported by their AFM topography images (Fig. S1). As shown in the size distribution histograms (insets of Fig. 1 a and b), the diameters of UCDs and ACDs are ∼4.1 nm and ∼3.8 nm, respectively. FTIR spectroscopy is performed to confirm the doping in these CDs (Fig. 1 c). The broad absorption peak at 3310 cm−1 in both the CDs can be attributed to -OH stretching, indicating the presence of carboxylate group, while UCDs shows an absorption band at 1637 cm−1, ascribed to C=O stretching. The presence of the amide group in ACDs is confirmed with the peaks at 1645 cm−1 (C=O stretching), 1381 cm−1, 1326 cm−1, and 1274 cm−1 (C-N stretching) [35]. Some other peaks at 1085 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1 are assigned to the C-N stretching of amines [36]. Such additional vibrational peaks corresponding to amide groups ensure nitrogen doping in ACDs compared to UCDs.

The optical properties of these CDs are investigated by using both steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy as given in the lower panel of Fig. 1. The absorption spectra of UCDs and ACDs (Fig. 1 d) show two excitonic peaks at ∼210 nm and ∼340 nm due to π-π* transition of the carbogenic core and n-π* transition of molecular states, respectively [37,38]. The PL emission maxima for UCDs and ACDs are observed to be ∼463 nm and ∼455 nm, respectively when excited at 370 nm (solid line in Fig. 1 e). The excitation spectra recorded at the corresponding PL maxima of UCDs and ACDs reveal the exact excitation wavelengths of 370 nm and 360 nm, respectively (dashed line in Fig. 1 e). The UCDs exhibit unique characteristics of excitation-dependent PL behavior (Fig. S2a), originating from the multiple carrier trap sites acting as multi-chromophoric units [39,40]. Contrastingly, the nitrogen doping in ACDs localizes these energy states forming a single excitonic transition and hence, showing excitation-independent PL behavior (Fig. S2b) [41]. To understand the excited state dynamics of UCDs and ACDs, their PL lifetime decays are recorded (Fig. 1 f) showing multi-exponential features (Eq. 1). The average lifetime values are computed from the fitting parameters using Eq. 2. The enhanced lifetime in ACDs (15.1 ns) than UCDs (4.32 ns) is attributed to the formation of new surface states with nitrogen doping, which can trap electrons and thus facilitate radiative recombination [42,43].

As per earlier work, semiconducting CDs can be employed to explore the ET process with a variety of small molecules [44]. Hutton et al. have reported an efficient ET mechanism between negatively charged enzymes and the positively charged ammonium-terminated CDs, whereas a weak Coulombic interaction with the negatively charged CDs diminishes the ET process [45]. While explaining the role of doping, Mondal et al. have reported an improved ET efficiency in phosphate and boron-doped CDs with redox mediator, methyl viologen [46]. However, the impact of solvent pH on this ET mechanism in CDs-based systems is yet to be fully explored. We have selected DA as the quencher molecule having interesting structural alteration by pH variation acting as both electron donor and acceptor. To investigate the interaction between these CDs and DA with its pH-induced structural variation, we have recorded the PL spectra and lifetime decay of CDs with the gradual addition of DA from 0 to 0.9 mM at pH-3 (acidic), 7 (neutral), and 10 (alkaline). A sequential drop in PL intensity of UCDs at different pH with an increase in the concentration of DA is observed at λex= 377 nm (insets of Fig. 2
a-c). In addition, it is evinced that the average lifetime of both UCDs (Fig. 2 a-c) and ACDs (Fig. 2 d-f) diminishes after the inclusion of DA, irrespective of the pH. For UCDs, the lifetime values are lowered from 4.39 ns to 3.06 ns at pH-3, 4.32 ns to 2.76 ns at pH-7, and 4.25 to 2.63 ns at pH-10 (Tables S1-S3). Along with the quenching in PL intensity (insets of Fig. 2 d-f), the average lifetime of ACDs decreases from 13.02 ns to 8.6 ns for pH-3, 15.1 ns to 9.07 ns for pH-7, and 14.47 ns to 8.49 ns for pH-10 with 0.9 mM of DA addition (Tables S4-S6). These outcomes can be ascribed to the ET process in these CDs with DA for all the three pH. The corresponding absorption spectra of both UCDs and ACDs are given in Fig. S3, in which the gradual rise in the optical density (O.D.) values after the addition of DA is contributed by the absorbance of DA itself (Fig. S4), irrespective of pH. As a control measurement, the pH dependency on the optical properties of these CDs is tested under different pH media (Fig. S5). The spectroscopic results of UCDs (Fig. S5a, b) show a minimal change in PL intensity and lifetimes (Table S7) with the change in solvent pH. The change in pH for ACDs does not affect their excitation and emission maxima (Fig. S5c), however, for the acidic medium, the PL intensity and average lifetime drop slightly from 15.1 ns to 13.02 ns, with insignificant changes from neutral to alkaline pH (Fig. S5d, Table S7). This may be accredited to the aggregation of ACDs after the protonation of amine moieties forming ammonium (NH3 +) salts in an acidic medium [47,48]. From the above outcomes, it is depicted that the alteration in pH does not affect the photo-physics of these CDs significantly, and can be ascribed as a consequence of the structural modification of DA with varied pH. We have further checked the stability of the as-prepared CDs with temperature and different ion concentrations. A comparison in the decrease in the PL intensity with a variation in temperature from 15°C to 60°C, infers that the ACDs have more thermal stability compared to UCDs (Fig. S6). Studies on incremental addition of salts like sodium chloride, magnesium chloride and heavy ions like europium acetate to both UCDs and ACDs, have negligible impact on their absorption and PL spectra, indicating exceptional photostability of the CDs in the presence of ions (Fig. S7 and S8).

To get insight into such an unabridged ET process, we have traced the S-V plot from PL intensity (Fig. 3
a, c) as well as average lifetime values (Fig. 3 b, d) by using Eqs. 3a and 3b. The S-V quenching constants (KSV ), as determined from the slope of τ0/τ vs [DA] plots indicate a faster ET rate in pH-10 than in pH-7 and 3 for both UCDs and ACDs (Table S8). The higher quenching rate for UCDs is observed in alkaline pH with KSV value of 66.9 M−1, followed by neutral (61.9 M−1) and acidic pH (49.4 M−1), respectively. Similar results are also obtained for ACDs, in which the highest KSV value is found for pH-10 (77.8 M−1) than pH-7 (68.0 M−1) and pH-3 (57.4 M−1). The nonlinearity in F0/F vs [DA] graph in all cases represents the presence of both static and collisional quenching. The calculated ET efficiency values (Eq. 4, Table S9) are also in line with the KSV values showing maximum ET efficiency for UCDs in pH-10 (38 %) than in pH-7 (36 %) and pH-3 (30 %). A similar trend of higher ET efficiency is also observed for ACDs in pH-10 (42 %), compared to pH-7 (40 %), and pH-3 (34 %).

The above discussion illustrates that alkaline pH significantly enhances the quenching rate as compared to acidic pH. The hydroquinone form of DA acts as a poor electron acceptor in acidic pH, hence, limiting the PL quenching ability of CDs [23]. In basic pH, DA-quinone becomes a favorable electron acceptor enhancing the PL quenching with additional non-radiative decay channels, resulting in decreased lifetime [49,50]. The mechanism behind the interaction between these CDs and DA in different pH media is represented in Scheme 1 a. From the S-V plots, it is evident that both the ET rate and efficiency are higher for ACDs compared to UCDs irrespective of the pH. This can be explained by the electrostatic interaction between ACDs and DA leading to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the -NH3 + moiety of DA and the corresponding surface ligand of ACDs as reported by Louleb et al. [51] They have observed a more stable complex due to the interaction of amide groups in DA with the oxygen moieties in -CH2CONH2 ligand of ACDs than that with the -CH2COOH ligand of UCDs [51], describing the higher quenching rate in ACDs than UCDs with DA (Schemes 1 b, 1 c).

While the ensembled spectroscopic measurements provide an intuitive understanding of the impact of DA structural variation on the photoinduced charge transfer with CDs, exploring its influence on local conductance at electrode-CD-electrode junction remains vital and yet challenging. The photo-driven ET rate in a donor and acceptor pair depends on the electronic coupling and modulates the Franck-Condon density of state [52]. Charge transport from one electrode to another in a single molecular study can be realized using the super-exchange charge transfer method [53]. Such single molecule-based measurements utilizing the C-AFM are ideal systems to investigate charge transport on the molecular scale for single electronic devices [54]. This specified technique not only provides the structural information of the molecule but also measures the local conductance at a molecular level by placing CDs between two metal electrodes (HOPG as the bottom electrode and PtIr AFM tip as the top electrode). The characteristic current (I)-voltage (V) curves for UCDs and ACDs in the absence (sphere) and presence (star) of the redox-active DA molecules at different pH are shown in Fig. 4
a-c and Fig. 4 d-f, respectively. All these curves exhibit a linear growth at lower bias whilst a sharp current increment is observed for higher applied bias voltage. Herein, ACDs are found to be more conducting compared to UCDs irrespective of the pH. This may be due to more carrier concentrations in ACDs than UCDs, as reflected in the lifetime measurements [55]. The enhanced conductivity of ACDs can be explained by higher conjugation in amides relative to the carboxylic acids [56] and strong binding between the lone pair of amines with the under-coordinated adatoms on the electrode surface [57].

The single molecular conductance of both CDs is greatly influenced by the change in pH and the presence of DA, as shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S9. The change in pH from neutral to acidic and alkaline or the insertion of DA enhances the conductivity of the system abruptly for both the CDs. This amplification in the conductivity for acidic and alkaline pH may be ascribed to the increase in the number of charge carriers, i.e., protons (acidic medium) and hydroxides (alkaline medium) in the system, respectively. Another aspect to describe these outcomes is the alteration in the local density of states at the Fermi level due to the addition of extra charge carriers after the surface modification of the CDs [6]. A similar increase in the conductivity at acidic pH is reported by Kalman et al. with regulated surface charges of the nanopores in nanofluidic bipolar transistors showing on/off switching applications [58]. Hong et al. have proposed that the probable decisive factors for higher conductivity of polyaniline films in acidic pH conditions are the degree of protonation of monomers and the early-stage polymerization [59]. It is interesting to note that the introduction of DA to these CDs enhances their conductivity (Fig. 4). This is because, the redox-active DA molecules can be reduced and oxidized reflecting structural switching from hydroquinone to quinone with a change in pH from an acidic to an alkaline medium [19,60]. In this hydroquinone/quinone system, the constructive interference in linearly-conjugated hydroquinone enhances the conductivity, while the cross-conjugated quinone exhibits lower conductance [61]. We have also monitored the I-V curve for only HOPG under identical conditions as a control experiment, displaying Ohmic characteristics (cyan, Fig. S10a). Additionally, the I-V traces are replicated 20 times for different sets, demonstrating the reproducibility of these results (Fig. S10, S11). A comparison of the I-V curves for UCDs and ACDs in the presence of DA with varied pH (Fig. S12) indicates that the increase in conductance is more for ACDs than UCDs. This finding is consistent with our spectroscopic results, which show that ACDs have higher ET efficiency than UCDs for all pH media (Table S9).

For an intuitive analysis of the conduction mechanism, several trials of fitting with different tunneling equations suggest a better fit for Simmon's model to these I-V curves (Eq. 5) [62]. This model is generally applied to the electron tunneling current through a thin energy barrier (<100 Å) in a metal-insulator-metal junction. The positive bias regions of these I-V data come up with a linear behavior in lower bias and non-linear growth at higher bias. From the initial linear portion, the conductance values of both the CDs in the absence and presence of DA in three different pH media are estimated (Table S10). This indicates the highest conductance in acidic medium (18.55 nS, 24.11 nS) compared to alkaline (7.34 nS, 9.14 nS) and neutral media (4.97 nS, 6.73 nS) for UCDs and ACDs, respectively, while a hike in the conductance is observed after DA addition to both the CDs. On the flip side, the non-linear current growth at higher voltage is fitted to same Simmon's model following the F-N type tunneling mechanism (Eq. 5b) [62]. When the applied bias voltage remains within the barrier height , the electrons tunnel directly through the trapezoidal barrier with linearly increasing current, showing direct tunneling. With a further increase in the voltage greater than the barrier height, the electron tunneling through the triangular barrier enhances the current non-linearly obeying the F-N tunneling [63,64].

To acquire a comprehensive idea of the charge transport in these CDs with DA, the I-V curves are re-plotted in a semilogarithmic scale as ln(I/V2) ∼ V−1 in the F-N plot (Fig. 5
, Eqs. 6a and 6b). The linear part at the higher bias region represents the F-N tunneling with the exponentially increasing direct tunneling part. The UCDs and ACDs display both the F-N and direct tunneling in neutral and alkaline pH media, whereas decreasing pH to acidic one trims the F-N part by tunneling directly. The transition from direct to F-N tunneling escorts a change in the shape of the barrier from trapezoidal to triangular and appears as an inflection point (Vtrans ) in the F-N plot [65]. For both UCDs and ACDs, Vtrans is lower for acidic (0.41 V, 0.33 V) and alkaline pH (0.49 V, 0.49 V) compared to neutral pH (0.55 V, 0.53 V), respectively (Table S11). The addition of DA decreases this value to 0.25 V, 0.19 V (acidic), 0.43 V, 0.31 V (alkaline), and 0.51 V, 0.36 V (neutral) for UCDs and ACDs, respectively. These above discussions conjecture that the larger barrier height and Vtrans in UCDs make them less conducting than ACDs while the interactions of DA with these CDs further reduce the barrier height, increasing their conductivity [14]. Again, the DA protonation and deprotonation with the pH variation in both the CDs accelerates the number of charge carriers which helps in perturbing their local conductance. The pH-induced charge transport in these CDs will help to design molecular-based nano-electronic devices.

The PL spectral features in conjunction with the conductance measurements reveal that the electron transfer and transport in undoped UCDs and doped ACDs are induced by the pH-dependent redox properties of DA. The combined steady-state and time-resolved PL studies suggest clear evidence of a faster ET process with increasing pH of the surrounding medium in both the CDs. This is attributed to the intriguing structural manipulation of DA enhancing its electron-accepting ability in basic pH. Such interconversion of the hydroquinone/quinone form of DA increases the local conductance of these CDs in a metal-CD-metal junction at acidic and alkaline pH compared to the neutral medium. This allows the charge carriers to tunnel the potential barrier directly at a low bias voltage in an acidic medium ceasing the F-N tunneling. Hence, a systematic improvement of the charge transfer and conduction towards the electrodes is pivotal and can disclose novel strategies in CDs-based sensing and optoelectronic applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: AP, PD, and SD acknowledge fellowships from IIT Patna. MKS acknowledges the funding received from the science and engineering research board (SERB) through project CRG/2022/004870. The authors also acknowledge FIST (SR/FST/PS-1/2018/34) for the AFM facility, Mr. Nishant Kumar, Ms. Anwesha Mahapatra, and Ms. Priyanka Priyadarshani Samal for their help in C-AFM measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2023.100198.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Wang, T.-W. Kim, G. Jo, T. Lee, Enhancement of field emission transport by molecular tilt configuration in metal−molecule−metal junctions, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131(16) (2009) 5980–5985.

ce:simple-para.ce:italic: I am an assistant professor in Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna having interest in charge transfer and transport of low dimensional semiconducting material like Carbon Dots, Perovskites and TMDs. The focus of our research stems for a better understanding of the various photoinduced excited state dynamics and their role in optoelectronic applications.
